title: Neurological Emergencies II — Seizures & Altered Mental Status
learningObjectives: List common causes of adult seizures and altered mental status.
Describe the postictal state and distinguish it from ongoing seizure activity.
Define status epilepticus and describe its field management priorities.
Apply a structured framework (AEIOU-TIPS) for reversible causes of altered mental status.
Describe field priorities for a seizing or newly altered adult patient.

summary.title: Not All Seizures Are Life-Threatening
summary.content: Most adult seizures are brief, self-limited events that stop on their own within a couple of minutes. Common causes include idiopathic epilepsy (a known seizure disorder, often with medication non-compliance as a trigger), hypoglycemia, head trauma, intoxication or withdrawal (especially alcohol withdrawal), fever (more classically pediatric but relevant in adults with underlying infection), stroke, and structural brain lesions. Field management for a brief, self-terminating seizure focuses on protecting the patient from injury and airway compromise during the event, not stopping a seizure that has already stopped.

summary.title: The Postictal State
summary.content: The postictal state is a period of transient confusion, drowsiness, and sometimes agitation that follows a seizure, typically resolving gradually over minutes without specific intervention. It is a normal, expected part of the seizure process, not a separate emergency — but a patient who does not gradually improve, or whose mental status worsens rather than clears, should prompt reconsideration of other causes (ongoing subclinical seizure activity, structural injury, or a coexisting problem like hypoglycemia).

summary.title: Status Epilepticus
summary.content: Status epilepticus — a seizure lasting 5 minutes or longer, or repeated seizures without return to baseline between them — is a time-critical emergency distinct from an ordinary brief seizure. It carries real risk of ongoing neuronal injury and cardiorespiratory compromise the longer it continues. Field priorities are airway protection/positioning, oxygen, glucose check, and rapid transport; benzodiazepine administration for status epilepticus follows local ALS protocol.

summary.title: AEIOU-TIPS: A Framework for Altered Mental Status
summary.content: AEIOU-TIPS is a widely taught mnemonic for reversible causes of altered mental status: Alcohol, Epilepsy/seizure (postictal), Insulin (hypo/hyperglycemia), Overdose/opioids, Uremia (metabolic), Trauma/temperature, Infection, Psychiatric, Stroke/shock. Its main clinical value is prompting a glucose check and a search for a reversible cause before assuming an irreversible or purely psychiatric explanation for a change in mental status.

summary.title: Field Priorities
summary.content: For an actively seizing patient: protect the airway with positioning and suction as needed (never place anything in the mouth), protect from injury, time the seizure, and check glucose as soon as feasible. For a newly altered but non-seizing patient: check glucose immediately, work through reversible causes, and gather a focused history (medications, known seizure disorder, substance use, recent illness or head injury) from bystanders or family. Any seizure activity lasting 5 minutes or longer, or that does not resolve with usual measures, should prompt escalation as status epilepticus per local protocol.

clinicalPearls: A brief seizure that has already stopped by EMS arrival does not need seizure-stopping medication — protect and reassess.
The postictal state should gradually improve — if it doesn't, look for another cause.
5 minutes is the threshold for status epilepticus — treat it as a time-critical emergency.
Check glucose in every altered or postictal patient — it's fast, safe, and can immediately explain the presentation.
redFlags: Seizure activity lasting 5 minutes or longer, or repeated seizures without return to baseline (status epilepticus).
Postictal confusion that worsens instead of gradually improving.
New focal neurologic deficit after a seizure that doesn't resolve as the postictal state clears (consider Todd's paralysis vs. stroke, but don't assume).
commonMistakes: Placing an object in the mouth of a seizing patient.
Assuming all confusion after a seizure is 'just postictal' without checking glucose.
Waiting well past 5 minutes of continuous seizure activity before escalating care.
memoryAids: AEIOU-TIPS: reversible causes of altered mental status.
Postictal should get better, not worse — a worsening trend means look further.
Protect, don't restrain, don't put anything in the mouth.

decisionTrees.title: Seizure and Altered Mental Status Field Approach
decisionTrees.content:
Seizure activity or altered mental status noted
  → If actively seizing: protect airway/position, protect from injury, time the seizure, check glucose
     <5 minutes, self-terminates → postictal care, monitor for gradual improvement, transport
     ≥5 minutes or repeated without return to baseline → Status epilepticus → time-critical emergency, escalate per local ALS protocol, rapid transport
  → If altered but not seizing: check glucose immediately, treat hypoglycemia if present
     Work through AEIOU-TIPS for reversible causes; gather focused history from bystanders/family
  → Reassess mental status trend — gradual improvement is reassuring, no improvement or worsening warrants continued investigation and rapid transport

references: General EMS/paramedic teaching on seizure classification and status epilepticus (5-minute threshold widely adopted in current EMS education)
AEIOU-TIPS mnemonic — standard EMS teaching framework for altered mental status
